questions.q: Which HTTP method is safe and idempotent?
What status code means the client is being rate-limited?
What header lets a client validate a cached response with the origin?
Which protocol does HTTP/3 run over?
What header compression format does HTTP/2 use?
Which real-time protocol is unidirectional from server to client?
What problem does HTTP/2 multiplexing solve?
questions.a: It allows multiple streams over one connection without serializing requests one after another.

questions.q: Why does HTTP/2 still suffer from head-of-line blocking?
questions.a: Because TCP still delivers one ordered byte stream underneath the multiplexed streams.

questions.q: How does a WebSocket connection begin?
questions.a: With an HTTP Upgrade request and response.

questions.q: What is the default serialization format in gRPC?
Why is HTTP/3 better at network changes than HTTP/2?
questions.a: QUIC connections are identified independently of the underlying network path.
The request succeeded but there is no response body.

questions.q: When would you pick SSE instead of WebSockets?
questions.a: When you only need server-to-client updates and want a simpler HTTP-based stream.
It avoids paying a full TCP handshake and slow-start cost for every request.

questions.q: Why is gRPC a poor fit for browsers compared with REST?
questions.a: Browsers do not natively expose the same gRPC transport and tooling model as simple HTTP APIs.

questions.q: Why can chunked transfer encoding help streaming responses?
questions.a: It lets the server send data before knowing the final content length.

questions.q: What tradeoff does HTTP/3 make by running over UDP?
questions.a: It reimplements reliability and congestion control in user space to gain flexibility.

questions.q: Why are idempotent methods important for retries?
questions.a: A client can safely retry them after a timeout without creating duplicate side effects.
